meta.title: Realtime feed leaks sockets and listeners
meta.summary: Route changes and filter changes keep adding listeners, so old listeners stay alive and still use old filter values.
signals: Events start showing up more than once after route or filter changes
Some duplicates still mention old filter values
Listener and subscription counts keep growing

context.symptom: The feed looks fine at first, but after opening and closing dashboards and changing filters a few times, each event appears multiple times.
context.userImpact: The page becomes noisy and slower over time, especially for users who navigate between dashboards.
context.environment: React page subscribes to a shared socket. The effect runs again whenever a new filters object is created.

evidence.title: Memory use
evidence.body: Every open-close cycle adds about 8MB of memory, and it never fully drops back.

evidence.title: Duplicate handler clues
evidence.body: After switching from 'all' to 'critical', some duplicate events still print the old 'all' filter value, which means an old handler is still alive.

evidence.title: Effect excerpt
Connection count
evidence.body: Sometimes there is only one socket connection, but the number of listeners and server subscriptions keeps climbing.

stages.title: Stage 1: likely root cause
stages.prompt: What is most likely causing the leak?
stages.helperText: Look for why old listeners and subscriptions are still alive.

options.label: The feed volume is just high enough that repeated messages look like a leak.
options.feedback: High traffic can look noisy, but it does not explain old handlers using the wrong filter value.

options.label: StrictMode double-invocation is the issue, so production cleanup does not matter.
options.feedback: StrictMode can reveal lifecycle issues, but the real problem is missing cleanup around external subscriptions.

options.label: Socket ownership is blurry: the effect reruns on filter changes, adds new listeners and subscriptions, and never fully tears down the old ones.
options.feedback: Correct. The main problem is ownership and cleanup, not just 'too many messages'.

options.label: The server is probably sending duplicates, so the client lifecycle is mostly fine.
options.feedback: Server duplicates are possible in real life, but old handlers using old filter values and the rising listener count still point back to the client.

stages.title: Stage 2: debug priority order
stages.prompt: What would you check first? Put the best three at the top.
stages.helperText: First prove what stays alive after route changes and filter changes.

candidates.label: Open and close the page, then change filters, while counting active listeners or subscriptions after each pass.
Check who owns the connection, what the effect depends on, and whether cleanup removes the exact handler it created.
Use logs to prove that old handlers still receive events with old filter values.
Compress socket payloads before checking lifecycle behavior.
Raise browser memory thresholds so the app stops slowing down.

stages.title: Stage 3: best fix set
stages.prompt: Which changes should be part of the first reliable fix?
stages.helperText: Pick the changes that make ownership clear and stop listeners from piling up.

options.label: Suppress duplicate message warnings in the console to keep the UI calm.
options.feedback: That hides the signal instead of fixing the retained subscriptions.

options.label: Return cleanup that unsubscribes and removes the exact handler the effect created before filters or route state changes.
options.feedback: Correct. Cleanup must remove the exact handler that was added.

options.label: Separate long-lived socket ownership from per-screen listeners so one layer owns the connection and screens only subscribe or unsubscribe.
options.feedback: Correct. One layer should own the socket. Screens should only subscribe and unsubscribe.

options.label: Create the socket in render so React always keeps it in sync with props.
options.feedback: Opening sockets during render makes lifecycle bugs worse, not better.

options.label: Narrow or stabilize dependencies so harmless filter object churn does not keep recreating listeners or subscriptions.
options.feedback: Correct. If the filters object becomes a new object for no real reason, the effect keeps rebuilding listeners.

stages.title: Stage 4: regression guard
stages.prompt: What is the best way to catch this again early?
stages.helperText: Pick the safeguard that exposes leaked listeners before users feel the slowdown.

options.label: Add a test that repeatedly opens and closes the page and changes filters, plus listener or subscription counts so leaks show up immediately.
options.feedback: Correct. It protects the exact messy path that caused the bug, not just memory in the abstract.

options.label: Have someone manually refresh the page monthly to watch for memory growth.
options.feedback: Manual checks are too weak for a lifecycle leak that can recur with small code changes.

options.label: Track only feed latency because slower messages caused the duplicates.
options.feedback: Latency is useful, but it does not catch duplicate listeners or retained sockets.

options.label: Force a full page reload after every navigation away from the feed.
options.feedback: That hides the leak by resetting the app instead of fixing subscription ownership.

scoreBands.label: You saw the slowdown, but not what stayed alive
scoreBands.summary: The app was slowing down, but the real problem was old listeners and subscriptions sticking around.

scoreBands.label: Right diagnosis, softer guardrail
scoreBands.summary: You found the cleanup issue, but the debug order or long-term guard still missed one important lever.

scoreBands.label: You fixed the ownership problem
scoreBands.summary: You named what stayed alive, removed the stale subscriptions, and protected both route changes and filter changes.

idealRunbook: Reproduce route changes and filter changes together.
Measure active listeners, subscriptions, and whether old handlers still fire.
Clarify who owns the socket and who only subscribes.
Add checks that make duplicate listeners impossible to miss.

teachingBlocks.title: Common interview trap
teachingBlocks.text: Do not stop at saying 'memory leak'. Name what stayed alive, who owned it, and why cleanup did not remove it.

teachingBlocks.title: Stronger explanation
items: Make it clear who owns the socket.
Cleanup removes the same handler that was added.
Dependencies are based on real connection changes, not every new object.

debrief.optionalReflectionPrompt: If one global socket stayed open for the whole app, where would you draw the boundary between connection ownership and per-screen subscriptions?
